content.text:
**#759 is merged and closed** — PR [#766](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/766) squash-merged, issue auto-closed, branch cleaned up, memory updated.

## What shipped

DataQ now **reads the dbt dependency graph into a `lineage_edges` cache and answers blast-radius questions**. Every dbt success (webhook = immediate, 10-min poll = fallback) dispatches a `refresh_dbt_lineage` Celery task that fetches `manifest.json` from the same artifact store #611 built, parses the v12 graph (ephemeral collapse, version gate, hardened against garbage), anchors it to the OpenLineage namespace via your existing assets (or an operator-pinned `lineage_namespace` on the dbt connection), and upserts assets + edges with connection-scoped pruning. `downstream_assets()`/`upstream_assets()` walk the graph depth-capped — the query #760's asset page and #761's incident evidence card will consume.

**Your refresh-propagation scenario is now a pinned regression test**: the real harness manifest (v1) → an edited v2 (edge added, edge removed, new mart) → the test proves new edges appear, the removed edge is pruned and vanishes from blast radius. Change lineage at the source, DataQ converges on the next build.

## The review round earned its keep again — 10 findings, all fixed in-PR

The three that genuinely mattered: the refresh originally ran **synchronously inside the API webhook handler** (seconds of artifact download + parsing blocking the ACK — now a Celery task); the prune could **corrupt a second dbt project's edges** when projects share assets (fixed with `connection_id` provenance on `lineage_edges` — which also simplified the prune); and the asset upsert **clobbered datasource provenance** on every refresh (now COALESCE-preserved). Plus: env-strict deterministic namespace anchoring with the operator escape hatch, adapter-aware canonicalization, a shared capped artifact loader that also hardened the pre-existing `run_results.json` path, batched upserts, and the fixtures trimmed from 1.3MB to ~80KB. Final gate: **1761 tests, 98.29% coverage**, all CI checks green, migration up/down/up verified.

**G-d scoreboard:** design #596 ✅ → asset entity #757 ✅ → OL emission #758 ✅ → dbt-manifest lineage #759 ✅. Remaining: **#760** (asset view — the read API/UI that surfaces all of this, plus exposing `asset_id` on the run/suite reads), **#761** (incidents), **#762** (LineageProvider/Marquez pull — where your Power BI scenario gets its answer). #760 is the natural next move; say the word.

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

(answer: only via catalog pull — Purview/DataHub — recorded on #762 with the "downstream nodes aren't always tables" seam contract).

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - DataQ: FastAPI + Celery + Redis + PostgreSQL/Alembic + GX Core; conda env `dataq`; repo /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ; local shell is fish (quote globs, no `timeout`, avoid `cd &&`, `===` breaks, use xargs not while-read).
   - ADR 0034: asset identity = OpenLineage dataset naming spec verbatim (namespace+name); lineage emitted/pulled, never built; incidents anchor to assets; asset/incident authz derived from suite grants.
   - OpenLineage naming: `snowflake://{org}-{account}` (fix_account_name: hyphen check scoped to **parts[0] only**; org-form returns first segment; locator+region gets `.aws`); `unitycatalog://{host}` IS a real spec row; flat-file asset = pattern base prefix; no case-folding in spec but engine-returned case (Snowflake upper-unquoted, UC lower).

class DbtConfig(BaseModel):
    """Non-secret dbt orchestration-connection config (credential comes from secrets).

    Maps from ``Connection.config``. ``project_name`` resolves a run to this
    connection (``resource_config_key``). ``artifacts_uri`` is the base location of

    ``<artifacts_uri>/<job>/latest/run_results.json`` for each name in ``jobs``.

    The per-connection secret is the artifacts-store read credential (ADLS SAS / S3
    secret key / unused for local). ``access_key_id``/``region`` are the non-secret
    S3 halves (required only for ``s3://``). The HMAC webhook signing key is a
    separate app-level secret (``settings.dbt_webhook_secret_name``), not here.
    """

    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")

    project_name: str
    artifacts_uri: str

    lineage_namespace: str | None = None

    @field_validator("artifacts_uri")

input.description: SF schema-drift live test
input.prompt:
You are running a LIVE schema-drift detection test for DataQ against the live production Snowflake account. You execute real DDL on Snowflake and drive the LOCAL DataQ stack (latest main, running at http://localhost:8000) end-to-end. Report evidence; do not modify any code.

## Context
- DataQ repo: /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ (READ-ONLY — never edit code; consult backend/app/ and the OpenAPI at http://localhost:8000/docs or /openapi.json for API shapes).
- Local stack is up: api :8000, worker, postgres (container dataq-postgres-1, db/user `dataq`), AUTH_DEV_BYPASS=true (verify auth shape via GET /api/v1/me — dev bypass likely accepts requests without a real token; read backend/app/api deps if unclear).
- DataQ today detects schema drift via GX schema-guard checks (`expect_table_columns_to_match_set`, `expect_column_values_to_be_of_type`, etc.) — the dedicated schema_drift monitor-kind (#592) is not built yet. Your job: prove the current detection path live.
- Scratchpad for ALL temp scripts: /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-arijit-Coding-Python-DataQ/aef9f6a4-f51b-4006-84c5-e914ce549fd6/scratchpad/sf-drift/ (mkdir it).

## Credentials — STRICT RULES
- NEVER print, echo, log, or write ANY secret to any file. Secrets flow only via process env / command substitution directly into the consuming command.
- Snowflake DDL creds: source the harness env inline: `sh -c 'set -a; . /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/secrets.sh; set +a; python your_script.py'` — the script reads SNOWFLAKE_ACCOUNT/SNOWFLAKE_USER/etc. from os.environ. SNOWFLAKE_ROLE there is ACCOUNTADMIN (fine for DDL). Do NOT cat/read secrets.sh contents into your transcript.
- DataQ connection secret (read-side): Key Vault `dataq-app-kv-aw6laj`, secret `conn-snowflake-retail` = a DATAQ_READER programmatic access token (PAT). Fetch inline: `sh -c 'SECRET=$(az keyvault secret show --vault-name dataq-app-kv-aw6laj -n conn-snowflake-retail --query value -o tsv) python post_connection.py'` — script reads SECRET from env and POSTs it in the JSON body to the local API. az is already logged in.
- Non-secret connection config halves (account identifier, user=DATAQ_READER, warehouse, database DATAQ_DB, role DATAQ_READER): read them from env names in secrets.sh via a script (without printing values is impossible for non-secrets — account/user/warehouse names are NON-secret identifiers, printing those is fine; only passwords/PATs/keys are secret) or from ~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/terraform/snowflake.tf. The prod DataQ Snowflake connection type's config schema: read backend/app/datasources/snowflake.py for exact field names.
- The Bash tool shell is FISH: wrap POSIX one-liners in `sh -c '...'`, don't use `VAR=x cmd` bare, no `timeout` on macOS, avoid `cd &&` chains. Python: use `conda run -n dataq python ...` (snowflake-connector-python is installed there) or run inside the api container (`docker exec dataq-api-1 sh -c 'cd /workspace && python ...'`).

## Test procedure
1. **Provision scratch objects on live Snowflake** (ACCOUNTADMIN via secrets.sh): `CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS DATAQ_DB.DRIFT_TEST`; table `DRIFT_TEST.DRIFT_CUSTOMERS(ID NUMBER, EMAIL VARCHAR, SIGNUP_TS TIMESTAMP_NTZ)`; insert ~20 sane rows; `GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA ... TO ROLE DATAQ_READER; GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA ... TO ROLE DATAQ_READER;` (also FUTURE TABLES grant — the type-change step recreates the table, which drops per-table grants).
2. **Wire DataQ**: POST a new Snowflake connection (name "drift-test-sf") to the local API with the reader PAT; run its test-connection endpoint — must pass. Create suite "Schema Drift — Snowflake (live)" targeting DRIFT_TEST.DRIFT_CUSTOMERS. Add schema-guard checks: (a) `expect_table_columns_to_match_set` with the exact 3-column set and exact_match true; (b) `expect_column_values_to_be_of_type` on ID (calibrate the dialect type name from the baseline run's observed value if it fails — e.g. DECIMAL/NUMBER); optionally (c) not-null on EMAIL as a control check that should stay green throughout.
3. **Baseline run**: trigger via POST /api/v1/suites/{id}/run, poll the run to terminal, fetch per-check results. ALL checks must pass. Record run id + outcomes.
4. **Drift 1 — column ADDED**: `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN LOYALTY_TIER VARCHAR`. Re-run suite → the column-set check must FAIL (observed set shows the extra column). Record evidence (observed_value from the check result).
5. **Drift 2 — column REMOVED**: `ALTER TABLE ... DROP COLUMN EMAIL`. Re-run → column-set check FAILS; note what happens to the EMAIL not-null control check (expected: it errors/fails since the column is gone — record the actual behavior faithfully, including whether DataQ surfaces a clear error vs a confusing one; that's a findings item, not something to hide).
6. **Drift 3 — type CHANGED**: recreate the table with ID as VARCHAR (`CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE ... (ID VARCHAR, ...)` + reinsert rows; re-grant SELECT if needed). Re-run → the type check on ID must FAIL. Record observed vs expected type.
7. **Cleanup**: `DROP SCHEMA DATAQ_DB.DRIFT_TEST CASCADE` on Snowflake. In local DataQ, delete the suite and the connection (verify deletes return 2xx).

## Deliverable (your final message)
A structured report: chronological table (action → run id → per-check outcomes), verdict per drift scenario (DETECTED / NOT DETECTED), exact observed_value evidence snippets, any DataQ bugs/UX gaps found (e.g. unclear errors on missing column), and confirmation that Snowflake + local-DataQ cleanup completed. Raw data, no fluff.

### DataQ bugs / UX gaps found

**1. (Bug, high severity) `check_id`↔result content misattribution when any check errors.** In both drift2 and drift3 runs, `GET /runs/{run_id}` returned `results[]` where each entry's `check_id` pointed to the *wrong* check's config/observed/expected values, in a consistent rotation. **3. (UX gap) Raw exception string surfaced as `observed_value.error`.** `"Error: The column \"status\" in BatchData does not exist."` is a raw pandas/GX exception message passed straight through to API consumers with no DataQ-level normalization (e.g. no structured `missing_column` reason code). Fine for an engineer, poor for an end-user-facing alert/dashboard.

**4. (Positive finding)** The `expect_column_values_to_be_of_type` "int64" guess for a UC `BIGINT` column worked correctly on the first try (no recalibration run needed) — useful precedent for future UC check authoring.

gh issue create -R TheurgicDuke771/DataQ \
  --title "Per-check results misattributed to wrong check_id when any check errors (GX reorders errored expectations first)" \
  --label bug --label priority/P1 --milestone "v1.1 Week 3 — Azure wind-down + local-first posture" \
  --body "## Summary

When any expectation in a run **errors** (e.g. its column no longer exists), the per-check \`results\` rows are attributed to the **wrong \`check_id\`s** — silent cross-wiring of result content (observed/expected values, error strings) across the suite's checks.

Found during the 2026-07-10 live schema-drift test against Unity Catalog (Free Edition) on latest main: dropping the \`status\` column made the not-null check error, and \`GET /runs/{run_id}\` then showed the not-null check's \"column does not exist\" error under the column-set check's id (and vice versa), consistently.

## Root cause (verified in code)

- GX 1.17.2 \`Validator.graph_validate\` builds its results in **two passes**: errored expectations are appended **first**, then the remaining pass/fail expectations in original order. So \`gx_result.results\` order ≠ submission order once anything errors.
- \`backend/app/datasources/gx_runner.py\` \`to_suite_outcome()\` copies \`gx_result.results\` order verbatim (no re-keying to the submitted specs).
- \`backend/app/services/run_service.py\` \`_run_all_checks\` explicitly relies on order (docstring: *\"outcome per check in the same order (so they zip 1:1 onto result rows)\"*) — \`zip(expectation_idx, suite_outcome.checks, strict=True)\` — and the persistence zip at ~L211 does the same.

Net: the order contract between the GX translation layer and the run service is broken exactly (and only) on the error path, which the happy-path tests never exercise.

## Repro

1. Suite with ≥2 expectation checks on a real table (e.g. \`expect_table_columns_to_match_set\` + \`expect_column_values_to_not_be_null\` on column X).
2. Drop column X in the datasource.
3. Run the suite → fetch \`GET /runs/{id}\` → the error result is attached to the wrong \`check_id\`.

Observed live in local runs \`0a7c1aa0-…\` and \`182cee1b-…\` (2026-07-10, UC drift-test scratch table since dropped; repro is deterministic from the steps above).

## Fix direction

Re-key GX results back to the submitted \`CheckSpec\`s in \`to_suite_outcome\` (GX result carries its \`expectation_config\` — match on type+kwargs or pass an index through \`meta\`), instead of trusting list order. Add a regression test where one of ≥2 expectations errors (missing column) and assert per-check attribution.

## Related (noted during the same test, lower severity)

- \`checks_total\` deliberately excludes \`error\`/\`skip\` tiers, so an errored run shows \`checks_total: 2\` with 3 result rows — by design (see \`check_outcome_counts\`), but combined with this bug the run summary is actively misleading.
- \`observed_value.error\` surfaces the raw GX/pandas exception string verbatim (\"Error: The column \\\"status\\\" in BatchData does not exist.\") — no DataQ-normalized reason code; fine for engineers, poor for alert/dashboard consumers.

Found by the live schema-drift test session (2026-07-10); test details in the session report." 2>&1

input.command:
gh issue create -R TheurgicDuke771/DataQ \
  --title "Check editor: expect_column_values_to_be_of_type needs dialect type guidance (Snowflake NUMBER ≠ \"NUMBER\")" \
  --label enhancement --label priority/P3 --milestone "v1.1 Backlog" \
  --body "Found in the 2026-07-10 live schema-drift test (Snowflake + UC).

Authoring \`expect_column_values_to_be_of_type\` against a Snowflake \`NUMBER\` column: the natural values \`type_: \"NUMBER\"\` and \`\"DECIMAL\"\` both FAIL — GX's SQL path does an exact (case-insensitive) string compare against the fully-qualified dialect type, which is \`DECIMAL(38, 0)\`. Two false-drift runs were needed before calibrating from the failing run's \`observed_value\`. (On UC/pandas the check compares Python value type names instead — \`int64\` worked first try for BIGINT, but a STRING column reports \`str\`, not \`object\`.)

A user writing the obvious type name gets a false schema-drift signal with no authoring-time hint.

Ideas (any one is enough):
- Check-editor helper text per datasource for this expectation type (\"use the exact engine type, e.g. DECIMAL(38, 0) — copy from a dry-run's observed_value\").
- Dry-run preview surfacing the observed type next to the input.
- Docs: a type-name cheat-sheet per datasource in the check-authoring guide.

Positive: \`observed_value\` on the failing result does show the exact expected string, so calibration is possible — just not discoverable." 2>&1 | tail -1

content.text:
All three live tests are done. **Both scenarios pass against the live production databases** — and there's one action that needs your go-ahead (a credential rotation the permission gate wouldn't let me run unattended).

## ⚠️ First: one action needed from you — rotate the UC PAT

The UC test agent made a handling mistake: it passed the live Databricks PAT (`conn-unity-catalog-retail`) as a CLI argument, and a `conda run` failure handler echoed the full command line — PAT included — into its local transcript. Exposure is confined to your machine, but per the #538 precedent it should be rotated. I wrote a full rotation script (mint new 90-day PAT with the old one → update Key Vault → revoke old → verify old is dead) at `scratchpad/rotate_uc_pat.py`, but the auto-mode permission gate blocked me from executing a Key Vault write you hadn't asked for. Say the word and I'll run it, or run it yourself:

```
sh -c 'OLD_PAT=$(az keyvault secret show --vault-name dataq-app-kv-aw6laj -n conn-unity-catalog-retail --query value -o tsv) conda run -n dataq python /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-arijit-Coding-Python-DataQ/aef9f6a4-f51b-4006-84c5-e914ce549fd6/scratchpad/rotate_uc_pat.py'
```

## Scenario 1 — Schema drift: 6/6 DETECTED (Snowflake 3/3, Unity Catalog 3/3)

Test setup: scratch tables on the live warehouses (`DATAQ_DB.DRIFT_TEST.DRIFT_CUSTOMERS`, `dataq_retail.drift_test.drift_orders`), a suite of GX schema-guard checks in the local stack (rebuilt on today's main), baseline runs green, then real DDL. Every drift class was flagged on the next run, on both engines: **column added** (column-set check failed showing the extra column in `observed_value`), **column removed** (column-set failed; the check on the missing column errored with a clear "column does not exist"), **type changed** (`NUMBER→VARCHAR` / `BIGINT→STRING` both caught by the type check). Note this is the GX-check detection path — the dedicated `schema_drift` monitor-kind is still #592 in Week 4.

The test earned its cost by surfacing a real bug: **[#767](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/767) (P1, filed to W3)** — when any check *errors*, GX 1.17 moves errored expectations to the front of its results list, but `run_service` zips results back onto checks positionally, so **per-check results get stamped with the wrong `check_id`** (the error shows up under a different check's history). Reproduced deterministically on both Snowflake and UC, root-caused to `gx_runner.to_suite_outcome` + the positional `zip` — fix is to re-key by expectation config. Also filed [#768](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/768) (backlog): authoring `expect_column_values_to_be_of_type` on Snowflake requires the exact engine string `DECIMAL(38, 0)` — "NUMBER" gives a false drift signal with no authoring-time hint.

## Scenario 2 — Lineage add/drop view: PASSED both acceptance criteria, no bugs

The full production pipeline ran live: a new dbt view `rpt_order_revenue_daily` (on `mart_order_revenue`) was added to the harness project, built against live Snowflake, artifacts uploaded to ADLS, and the dbt provider poll → `refresh_dbt_lineage` picked it up — **the view appeared as a transitive downstream of the monitored `RETAIL.ORDER_LINES` asset** (`order_lines → stg_order_lines → mart_order_revenue → rpt_order_revenue_daily`), edges 8→9. Then the model was removed, the view dropped in Snowflake, artifacts republished — **the edge was pruned and the view vanished from the blast radius**, edges back to 9→8. Refresh latency was ~330ms from poll to updated cache. Bonus proof: the ORDER_LINES asset created by a suite run and the one the dbt refresh resolved were the **same row** (identity unification + provenance preservation working as designed).

## State left behind

Snowflake and UC scratch schemas are dropped and verified gone; the harness dbt project is byte-identical to before; the DataQ repo untouched. The local stack keeps the two lineage test connections (`lineage-sf-retail`, `lineage-dbt-project`) — useful for #760/#761 work, but the dbt one will produce fail-soft poll noise once the ADLS SAS dies at the Azure wind-down; tell me if you want them deleted. One doc-worthy footgun from the lineage run: the dbt connection needs `artifacts_uri=adls://…/raw` + `jobs=["dbt"]`, not the harness's `DBT_ARTIFACTS_URI` value verbatim — I noted it in memory and can fold it into `docs/orchestration.md` with the #760 work.

- Register a **dbt connection** (Connections → dbt) with its `project_name`, the `jobs` it

input.new_string:
- Register a **dbt connection** (Connections → dbt) with its `project_name`, the `jobs` it
  publishes, and the `artifacts_uri` where builds land (`adls://…`, `s3://…`, or `file://…`)
  plus the store's read credential.
  > **`artifacts_uri` is the base prefix, not the full published path** — DataQ appends
  > `/<job>/latest/…` itself. If your publisher writes
  > `adls://<account>/<container>/<prefix>/dbt/latest/run_results.json`, register
  > `artifacts_uri: adls://<account>/<container>/<prefix>` with `jobs: ["dbt"]`. Pasting the
  > publisher's own output variable verbatim usually double-counts the job segment (the poll
  > then looks in `<prefix>/dbt/dbt/latest/` and finds nothing).
